**Associazione Calcistica Perugia Calcio**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) or simply **Perugia**, is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Perugia](/wiki/Perugia "Perugia"), [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria"), Italy, that competes in the [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C") Group B, the third division of Italian football.

Founded in 1905 as Associazione Calcistica Perugia, the club folded in 2005 and were re-founded the same year as Perugia Calcio, before dissolving once again in 2010, taking on its current name.

The club has played 13 times in the Serie A; their best placement was finishing as unbeaten runners-up in the 1978–79 season, becoming the first team under the round-robin format to finish a Serie A season without defeats. In addition to various minor league titles, the club has won the [2003 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2003_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2003 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), and has made two [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") appearances. In its Serie A spell under club president [Luciano Gaucci](/wiki/Luciano_Gaucci "Luciano Gaucci") around the turn of the century Perugia had some upset wins at home, most notably against [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") on the final day in [2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Serie_A "1999–2000 Serie A"), which led to their opponents dropping the title win to [Lazio](/wiki/SS_Lazio "SS Lazio"). Gaucci's era ended with relegation in [2004](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Serie_A "2003–04 Serie A") after which bankruptcy unfolded.

The club's players are nicknamed "biancorossi" (red and whites) due to their historical kit colours, which include red shirts and socks accompanied by white shorts, and "grifoni" (griffins), inspired by their city's heraldic symbol. They play their home matches at the 28,000-capacity [Stadio Renato Curi](/wiki/Stadio_Renato_Curi "Stadio Renato Curi"). In the 1979–80 season, they became the first Italian football team to show a kit sponsorship.[[5]](#cite_note-ponte-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### A.C. Perugia (early years)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia (early years)")]

A.C. Perugia were founded on 9 June 1905, after the merger of *U.S. Fortebraccio* and *Libertas*.[[6]](#cite_note-6) During the early 1900s, the club primarily engaged in regional competitions.[[7]](#cite_note-:Amore-7)
Throughout the 1930s, Perugia experienced significant growth, culminating in its inaugural promotion to Serie B in 1933. Notably, in 1937, Perugia constructed its first official stadium, Santa Giuliana. After a second promotion to Serie B in the season 1945–46, Perugia spent almost all of the 1950s in the lower divisions.[[7]](#cite_note-:Amore-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Associazione_Calcio_Perugia_1933-34.jpg/500px-Associazione_Calcio_Perugia_1933-34.jpg)](/wiki/File:Associazione_Calcio_Perugia_1933-34.jpg)

1933–34 Perugia line-up

### A.C. Perugia (1960s-1975)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia (1960s-1975)")]

The 1960s marked a resurgence for Perugia, under the presidency of prominent local entrepreneur Lino Spagnoli. The promotion to Serie B in the [1966-1967 season](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Serie_C "1966–67 Serie C") would mark the beginning of one of the club's most successful periods.[[7]](#cite_note-:Amore-7) Perugia spent the next eight years in Serie B before promotion to Serie A for the first time in 1975.[[8]](#cite_note-:Undefeated-8)
The society underwent profound renewal, with the arrival of Apulian entrepreneur [Franco D'Attoma](/wiki/Franco_D%27Attoma "Franco D'Attoma") as president and with a new technical and managerial staff, including [Ilario Castagner](/wiki/Ilario_Castagner "Ilario Castagner"), a former player for the team in the early part of the previous decade, as coach, and [Silvano Ramaccioni](/wiki/Silvano_Ramaccioni "Silvano Ramaccioni") as sporting director. Several new players joined the team, including defender [Pierluigi Frosio](/wiki/Pierluigi_Frosio "Pierluigi Frosio"), midfielders [Franco Vannini](/wiki/Franco_Vannini "Franco Vannini") and [Renato Curi](/wiki/Renato_Curi "Renato Curi"), and forward [Paolo Sollier](/wiki/Paolo_Sollier "Paolo Sollier").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Sollier gained attention beyond football circles and was known for using a [clenched fist](/wiki/Clenched_fist "Clenched fist") salute on the pitch.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)
Perugia secured promotion to Serie A with a three-point lead over [Como](/wiki/Como_1907 "Como 1907") and a four-point lead over [Verona](/wiki/Hellas_Verona_FC "Hellas Verona FC").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/1974%E2%80%9375_Associazione_Calcio_Perugia.jpg/500px-1974%E2%80%9375_Associazione_Calcio_Perugia.jpg)](/wiki/File:1974%E2%80%9375_Associazione_Calcio_Perugia.jpg)

1974–75 Perugia first-team squad

### A.C. Perugia (D'Attoma years)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia (D'Attoma years)")]

The newly promoted Perugia, competing in the new [Comunale di Pian di Massiano stadium](/wiki/Stadio_Renato_Curi "Stadio Renato Curi"), embarked on its inaugural Serie A campaign, retaining much of the squad from the previous season. Their debut match in Serie A was against [Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") on October 5, 1975.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/1975%E2%80%9376_Serie_A_-_Perugia_v_AC_Milan.jpg/500px-1975%E2%80%9376_Serie_A_-_Perugia_v_AC_Milan.jpg)](/wiki/File:1975%E2%80%9376_Serie_A_-_Perugia_v_AC_Milan.jpg)

Serie A 1975-76 AC Perugia vs AC Milan

On May 16, 1976, Renato Curi - who played a pivotal role in Perugia's journey to Serie A - scored the decisive goal in the Perugia-Juventus match, sealing [Torino](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC")'s victory in the championship against Juventus.[[15]](#cite_note-Curi-15) Additionally, Perugia made its debut in European competitions during that season, participating in the [Mitropa Cup](/wiki/Mitropa_Cup "Mitropa Cup").[[15]](#cite_note-Curi-15)
Curi's performances and goals were crucial in securing Perugia's commendable sixth-place finish in the following season, making them the highest-ranked team excluded from UEFA qualification. Tragically, the talented Curi died on October 30, 1977, from a heart attack during a home match against Juventus, leaving a lasting impact on the club and leading to the stadium's renaming in his honor.[[15]](#cite_note-Curi-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Despite this loss, Perugia continued to excel. The following season, they made history by becoming the first team to complete a Serie A campaign undefeated, finishing runners-up in 1979 with 11 wins and 19 draws, resulting in the only unbeaten side not to win a title.[[8]](#cite_note-:Undefeated-8) Perugia's unbeaten record in the Serie A lasted for 37 matches from April 1978 until October 1979.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[8]](#cite_note-:Undefeated-8) Led by Castagner, the team's cohesive performance saw them finish second in the league standings behind AC Milan, earning widespread admiration as the "[Perugia of miracles](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_AC_Perugia_season "1978–79 AC Perugia season")." Among the key figures in their ranks were captain Pierluigi Frosio, along with Franco Vannini, [Antonio Ceccarini](/wiki/Antonio_Ceccarini "Antonio Ceccarini"), and [Salvatore Bagni](/wiki/Salvatore_Bagni "Salvatore Bagni").[[8]](#cite_note-:Undefeated-8)

Ahead of the [1979–80 season](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Serie_A "1979–80 Serie A"), Perugia ambitiously signed Italy national team striker [Paolo Rossi](/wiki/Paolo_Rossi "Paolo Rossi") from [L.R. Vicenza](/wiki/LR_Vicenza "LR Vicenza").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Also notable was President D'Attoma's pioneering commercial deal with the Ponte pasta factory, which marked the first instance of a kit sponsorship for an Italian football team, setting a precedent in the history of Italian football.[[5]](#cite_note-ponte-5)

Despite Rossi's performance as a goal scorer, the team couldn't replicate their previous success, seeming overwhelmed by newfound fame. Perugia struggled in the league and their first [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") appearance ended early in the round of 16 against [Aris Thessaloniki](/wiki/Aris_Thessaloniki_F.C. "Aris Thessaloniki F.C.").[[19]](#cite_note-UEFA-1979-19) The ongoing absence of a key midfielder like Vannini, who later retired due to injury, further hindered their season.[[20]](#cite_note-Gazzetta-20)

However, as the club appeared to have solidified its position in Italian football, an unexpected turn of events occurred. In March 1980, the [Totonero](/wiki/1980_Totonero "1980 Totonero") scandal erupted, implicating Rossi himself and indirectly impacting Perugia's fate. The team collapsed, finishing the season in a nondescript seventh place and was penalized five points in the following season, which ultimately led to relegation in 1981.[[8]](#cite_note-:Undefeated-8)

### A.C. Perugia (1980s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia (1980s)")]

The club spent the first half of the 1980s trying to get back to Serie A, nearly succeeding under the management of [Aldo Agroppi](/wiki/Aldo_Agroppi "Aldo Agroppi") in the [1984-1985 season](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_Serie_B "1984–85 Serie B"), narrowly missing promotion back to Serie A by just one point, setting records for the fewest defeats (1) and most draws (26 out of 38 matches) in the Serie B, a sign of potential resurgence.[[21]](#cite_note-21) However, the club's decline continued, culminating in a double relegation to Serie C2 in 1986 due to their involvement in the [1986 Totonero](/wiki/1986_Totonero "1986 Totonero") scandal, despite already facing relegation on the pitch.[[22]](#cite_note-22) It was during this time that [Fabrizio Ravanelli](/wiki/Fabrizio_Ravanelli "Fabrizio Ravanelli") would be discovered, he would later go on to a career with Reggiana, Juventus, Middlesbrough and several other clubs before returning to Perugia.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### A.C. Perugia (Gaucci years)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia (Gaucci years)")]

In 1991, the entrepreneur and sportsman [Luciano Gaucci](/wiki/Luciano_Gaucci "Luciano Gaucci") took control of the club.[[24]](#cite_note-:Gaucci-24) His impactful legacy with the team endured until 2005, characterized by notable moments, including the contentious attempt to recruit the first female footballer for a male Serie A side, an unexpected victory against championship contender Juventus in 2000, and the triumph in the Intertoto Cup in 2003.[[24]](#cite_note-:Gaucci-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Gaucci, known for his unconventional methods of dealing with players, coaches, and journalists, expressed his determination to swiftly return the Biancorossi to the top flight, a goal he achieved within five years.[[24]](#cite_note-:Gaucci-24) To accomplish this, Gaucci initiated significant transfer campaigns in the early 1990s, making notable signings such as [Giuseppe Dossena](/wiki/Giuseppe_Dossena "Giuseppe Dossena") and [Giovanni Cornacchini](/wiki/Giovanni_Cornacchini "Giovanni Cornacchini").[[26]](#cite_note-:Gaucci-2-26) Despite narrowly missing promotion in the 1991–1992 season, Perugia secured promotion to Serie B the following year after defeating Acireale in a playoff match. However, the joy was short-lived as Gaucci was embroiled in a scandal involving a gift of a horse to the family of a favorable referee, leading to the denial of Serie B promotion by the Italian Football Federation.[[27]](#cite_note-:Gaucci-3-27) Nevertheless, the 1993–1994 season saw Perugia, led by [Ilario Castagner](/wiki/Ilario_Castagner "Ilario Castagner"), dominate their group and earn promotion to Serie B, with Cornacchini clinching the top scorer title for the second consecutive year.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The team's stay in Serie B lasted only two seasons, as they achieved promotion to Serie A in the 1995–1996 season under the guidance of [Giovanni Galeone](/wiki/Giovanni_Galeone "Giovanni Galeone"). Led by captain [Federico Giunti](/wiki/Federico_Giunti "Federico Giunti") and striker [Marco Negri](/wiki/Marco_Negri "Marco Negri"), Perugia secured promotion with a dramatic victory over Verona, ultimately sealing their return to the top flight after a fifteen-year absence.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Perugia started well before Gaucci's decision to replace Galeone with [Nevio Scala](/wiki/Nevio_Scala "Nevio Scala").[[30]](#cite_note-A-1997-30) The side's form subsequently declined before a late rally gave them a chance of survival- a 2–1 defeat at Piacenza in the final round ultimately determined relegation by tie-breaker rules due to finishing level on points.[[30]](#cite_note-A-1997-30) With Castagner back in charge, Perugia won a play-off with Torino to secure a return to the top flight.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The next six seasons saw Perugia hold their own in Serie A with foreign imports including the Japanese international [Hidetoshi Nakata](/wiki/Hidetoshi_Nakata "Hidetoshi Nakata") in 1998 and the Ecuadorian top goal scorer Ivan Kaviedes.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The team came under scrutiny when Gaucci criticised and eventually terminated the contract of his own player, [Ahn Jung-Hwan](/wiki/Ahn_Jung-Hwan "Ahn Jung-Hwan") of South Korea, for scoring the [golden goal](/wiki/Golden_goal "Golden goal") that knocked Italy out of the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), and allegedly insulting the Italian nation. Ahn's national manager [Guus Hiddink](/wiki/Guus_Hiddink "Guus Hiddink") spoke out against the sacking.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the summer of 2003, Perugia signed English striker [Jay Bothroyd](/wiki/Jay_Bothroyd "Jay Bothroyd"), and [Al-Saadi Gaddafi](/wiki/Al-Saadi_Gaddafi "Al-Saadi Gaddafi") (the son of Libyan dictator [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi")).[[34]](#cite_note-34) Soon after, the club were one of three winners of the [2003 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2003_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2003 UEFA Intertoto Cup") after beating [VfL Wolfsburg](/wiki/VfL_Wolfsburg "VfL Wolfsburg") of Germany 3–0 on aggregate. This qualified the team to the [2003–04 UEFA Cup](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_UEFA_Cup "2003–04 UEFA Cup"), in which they were eliminated in the third round by [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Perugia Calcio (2005–2010)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Perugia Calcio (2005–2010)")]

The new chairman Vincenzo Silvestrini had re-established the club in 2005 as *Perugia Calcio*.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

After a takeover, in 2009 Perugia Calcio property passed to Perugian entrepreneur and former [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa_S.C. "Pisa S.C.") owner and chairman Leonardo Covarelli. On 21 May 2010 the Court of Perugia declared the bankruptcy of Perugia Calcio srl.[[37]](#cite_note-crack_2010-37) Nobody decided to take over the society at the subsequent auction[[38]](#cite_note-38) and on 30 June 2010 the club was unable to join the Italian third level championship 2010–2011. The [Italian Football Federation](/wiki/Italian_Football_Federation "Italian Football Federation") decided on 8 July 2010 to revoke the affiliation of the bankrupt *Perugia Calcio Srl*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### A.C. Perugia Calcio (2010–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AC_Perugia_Calcio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: A.C. Perugia Calcio (2010–present)")]

In July 2010, a new club with the same denomination as ASD Perugia Calcio and inheriting the old side history, was entered into the [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D") with local entrepreneur Roberto Damaschi as chairman.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

On 10 April 2011, Perugia became the first team of the season to get promoted from Serie D to the [Lega Pro Seconda Divisione 2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Lega_Pro_Seconda_Divisione "2011–12 Lega Pro Seconda Divisione"), after a 3–2 home victory against [Castel Rigone](/wiki/A.S._Castel_Rigone "A.S. Castel Rigone").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The club also won the 2010–11 [Coppa Italia Serie D](/wiki/Coppa_Italia_Serie_D "Coppa Italia Serie D"), beating [Turris](/wiki/F.C._Turris_1944_A.S.D. "F.C. Turris 1944 A.S.D.") 1–0 in the final.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In summer 2011 the club was renamed *Associazione Calcistica Perugia Calcio*, to play in the [Lega Pro Seconda Divisione/B](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Lega_Pro_Seconda_Divisione#Girone_B "2011–12 Lega Pro Seconda Divisione") obtaining immediate promotion to [Lega Pro Prima Divisione](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "Lega Pro Prima Divisione").[[43]](#cite_note-43) On 4 May 2014, beating Frosinone 1–0, AC Perugia won the 2013–14 Lega Pro Prima Divisione championship and gained promotion to Serie B after a 9-year absence from Italy's second highest football division. Additionally, Roman entrepreneur Massimiliano Santopadre acquired full ownership of the club's shares.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

On 2 May 2021, Perugia finished in first place in group B of the [2020–21 Serie C](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Serie_C "2020–21 Serie C"), and were promoted back to the Serie B.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45) Their promotion came after consecutive wins in the last five games on the season, having been in third place, six points from first place.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45) The club was relegated back to [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C") at the end of the [2022–23 Serie B](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Serie_B "2022–23 Serie B") season.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
